era_context: 1375 is a year of relative stability and consolidation across multiple civilizations. In Western Europe, Edward III of England is aging but still ruling, and the Hundred Years' War continues with reduced intensity. In China, the Ming Dynasty is consolidating power under the Hongwu Emperor. In the Ottoman Empire and the Balkans, Ottoman expansion continues. In Italy, the early Renaissance is beginning to flourish. This is a year of cultural and political development before the major transitions of 1376–1378.

events.title: Hundred Years' War: English military operations and truces
events.description: The Hundred Years' War continued in 1375 with English forces conducting military operations in France. The war had entered a phase of reduced intensity compared to the mid-14th century, with a series of truces, raids, and limited engagements rather than large-scale battles. In 1375, the English were attempting to maintain their territorial holdings in Aquitaine and Calais while conducting raids along the Channel coast. The war was characterized by a series of truces and negotiations, with neither side achieving decisive victory. The Treaty of Bruges (1375) established a truce between England and France that would last until 1378.
key_figures: Charles V of France (King of France)
events.certainty_note: The Treaty of Bruges is documented in contemporary diplomatic records and is attested in multiple chronicles. The broader military context is well-documented.

events.title: Ming Dynasty: Hongwu Emperor's consolidation of power
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang) continued his systematic consolidation of Ming power in 1375. The emperor was implementing administrative reforms designed to centralize authority and eliminate the power of regional warlords and the old Yuan bureaucracy. In this year, the Ming court was reorganizing provincial administration and establishing the institutional structures that would define Ming governance for centuries. The Hongwu Emperor was also consolidating military power and establishing the system of imperial examinations that would become central to Ming administration. By 1375, the Ming Dynasty had been in power for seven years and had largely eliminated remaining Yuan loyalists.
key_figures: Zhu Yuanzhang / Hongwu Emperor (Emperor of China)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: Ming administrative records and the official Ming Shilu provide detailed documentation of the Hongwu Emperor's reign and consolidation efforts.

events.title: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans: Consolidation of territorial gains
events.description: The Ottoman Empire under Murad I continued its expansion into the Balkans in 1375. Ottoman forces were consolidating control over territories conquered in previous years and preparing for further expansion. The Ottoman military system, based on the devshirme (levy of Christian boys for military service) and the elite Janissary corps, was becoming increasingly effective. By 1375, the Ottoman Empire was the dominant military power in the eastern Mediterranean and the Balkans, and Christian states in the region were increasingly fragmented and unable to mount effective resistance.
events.certainty_note: The broader context of Ottoman expansion is well-established, though specific events in 1375 are not well-documented in contemporary sources. Ottoman chronicles are often later compilations with uncertain dating.

events.title: Early Italian Renaissance: Cultural flourishing in Florence and other city-states
events.description: The early Italian Renaissance was beginning to flourish in 1375, particularly in Florence and other Italian city-states. This period saw the development of new artistic styles, the revival of classical learning, and the patronage of the arts by wealthy merchant families such as the Medici. In 1375, the cultural foundations of the Renaissance were being laid, with artists, writers, and scholars beginning to develop new approaches to art, literature, and philosophy. The period was characterized by a renewed interest in classical antiquity and a move away from medieval artistic and intellectual traditions. Key figures included Petrarch (who died in 1374) and his intellectual heirs, who were promoting the study of classical texts and the development of humanism.
key_figures: Florentine merchant families (patrons of the arts)

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on the Italian Renaissance
Contemporary Italian chronicles and literary works

events.certainty_note: The broader context of the early Italian Renaissance is well-established by modern scholarship, though specific events in 1375 are not as thoroughly documented as later periods. The dating of the 'beginning' of the Renaissance is somewhat arbitrary and debated by scholars.

events.title: Papal politics: Gregory XI consolidates papal authority
events.description: Pope Gregory XI (Pierre Roger) continued his efforts to consolidate papal authority in 1375. Gregory XI had been elected pope in 1370 and was working to strengthen papal power and reform the Church. In 1375, the papal court was experiencing significant political tensions related to the question of the papal seat's location (Avignon vs. Rome) and the future direction of the Church. Gregory XI was also dealing with the Papal States' political and military challenges, including conflicts with various Italian city-states and the threat posed by the Ottoman Empire in the eastern Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: The broader context of papal politics is well-documented, though specific events in 1375 are not as thoroughly documented as later years.

disconfirming_evidence: The Treaty of Bruges (1375) is sometimes presented as a major turning point in the Hundred Years' War, but modern scholarship emphasizes that it was one of many truces and that the war continued with similar intensity after 1378. The claim that the early Italian Renaissance 'began' in 1375 is somewhat arbitrary; modern scholars debate the precise dating of the Renaissance's beginning, with some placing it earlier (14th century) and others later (15th century). The significance of 1375 as a specific turning point in cultural history is somewhat overstated.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1375 is moderately rich for Western Europe, particularly regarding the Hundred Years' War and papal politics, which are documented in chronicles and diplomatic records. The record is detailed for China (Ming administrative records) and sparse for the Ottoman Empire and the Balkans, where Ottoman sources are often later compilations with uncertain dating. The record is minimal for sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, the Americas, and Oceania. The year is well-documented for elite political and military history but poorly documented for economic, social, and cultural history outside elite circles and the early Renaissance. There is a significant Western bias in the surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: The Treaty of Bruges (1375) established a truce that lasted until 1378, when the Hundred Years' War resumed
The early Italian Renaissance that was beginning in 1375 would flourish throughout the 15th and 16th centuries
The papal tensions in 1375 led to the move of the papal seat to Rome in 1377 and the Great Schism in 1378
